probe_designs.content: Compute AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, and Brier score separately for each of the 6 ethnicity subgroups. Compare against the overall population metrics and measure disparity (max-min gap, standard deviation across groups). Healthy signal: metric gaps across groups within 1-2% and confidence intervals overlap. Unhealthy signal: large (>5%) AUROC/AUPRC gaps particularly for minority groups (e.g., Hispanic, Asian, Other), indicating systematic disparity. Also compute equalized odds and demographic parity gaps.
possible_sources: Chen et al. 'Ethical Machine Learning in Healthcare' (Annual Review 2021)
Röösli et al. 'Peeking into a black box, the fairness and generalizability of a MIMIC-III benchmarking model' (Scientific Data 2022)
Pfohl et al. 'An empirical characterization of fair ML for clinical risk prediction' (JBI 2021)
AIF360 toolkit (IBM)
Fairlearn

probe_designs.content: Assess whether predicted mortality probabilities reflect true mortality rates. Compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), and plot reliability diagrams overall and per-ethnicity. Fit Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test. Healthy signal: ECE < 0.05, calibration curves near diagonal for all subgroups. Unhealthy signal: systematic over/under-prediction in specific groups, indicating miscalibrated risk scores that could lead to biased clinical decisions. Post-hoc remedies: Platt scaling or isotonic regression per-subgroup.
possible_sources: Guo et al. 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Naeini et al. 'Obtaining Well Calibrated Probabilities Using Bayesian Binning' (AAAI 2015)
Barda et al. 'Addressing bias in prediction models by improving subpopulation calibration' (JAMIA 2021)

probe_designs.probe_name: Class Imbalance and Subgroup Representation Audit
probe_designs.content: Examine the training set for (a) mortality prevalence (positive class ratio, typically ~10-13% in MIMIC ICU) and (b) distribution of the 6 ethnicity groups. Check for severe underrepresentation (e.g., <5% of cohort). Evaluate effect of oversampling/class-weighting on both overall and subgroup metrics. Healthy signal: balanced loss contribution per group; minority groups have sufficient samples for reliable evaluation (>100 positive examples). Unhealthy signal: minority groups have too few samples, causing wide CI and unstable fairness metrics.
possible_sources: Johnson et al. 'MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database' (Scientific Data 2016)
Harutyunyan et al. 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series data' (Scientific Data 2019)
Buda et al. 'A systematic study of the class imbalance problem in CNNs' (Neural Networks 2018)

probe_designs.probe_name: Training/Validation Loss Curves and Overfitting Analysis
probe_designs.content: Monitor binary cross-entropy loss on train vs validation across epochs. Check for divergence (train loss decreasing while val loss plateaus/increases), which signals overfitting exacerbated by the high-dimensional TF-IDF input (10000 features). Plot loss per ethnicity subgroup to detect differential overfitting. Healthy signal: train/val curves track closely, val loss smoothly decreases then plateaus. Unhealthy signal: early divergence or loss oscillation; consider stronger L1/L2, dropout, or early stopping.
possible_sources: Prechelt 'Early Stopping - But When?' (Neural Networks 1998)
Goodfellow et al. 'Deep Learning' textbook Ch. 7
Caruana et al. 'Overfitting in neural nets: Backpropagation, conjugate gradient, and early stopping' (NIPS 2000)

probe_designs.content: Evaluate performance using stratified k-fold CV (stratified by both outcome and ethnicity) and, if data permits, temporal splits (train on earlier admissions, test on later) or hospital-unit splits. Report mean±std across folds. Healthy signal: low variance across folds (<0.02 AUROC std), robust performance on temporal holdout. Unhealthy signal: large fold variance indicates unstable fit; temporal degradation suggests concept drift in clinical notes or coding practices.
possible_sources: Futoma et al. 'The myth of generalisability in clinical research and machine learning in health care' (Lancet Digital Health 2020)
Nestor et al. 'Feature Robustness in Non-stationary Health Records' (MLHC 2019)
Harutyunyan et al. (Scientific Data 2019)

probe_designs.content: Test model stability by (a) dropping random subsets of TF-IDF features, (b) adding Gaussian noise to feature vectors, (c) ablating the ethnicity one-hot input. Compare AUROC pre/post perturbation. Also train with ethnicity input permuted/removed to measure its causal contribution to disparities. Healthy signal: graceful degradation; ethnicity removal does not collapse performance but may reduce fairness gap. Unhealthy signal: model relies heavily on ethnicity feature for predictions, indicating proxy use rather than clinically grounded reasoning.
possible_sources: Ribeiro et al. 'Why Should I Trust You? (LIME)' (KDD 2016)
Adebayo et al. 'Sanity Checks for Saliency Maps' (NeurIPS 2018)
Zhang et al. 'Hurtful words: quantifying biases in clinical contextual word embeddings' (CHIL 2020)

probe_designs.probe_name: TF-IDF Feature Importance and Clinical Plausibility
probe_designs.content: Extract top predictive n-grams using logistic regression coefficients, SHAP, or permutation importance. Check whether top features correspond to clinically meaningful terms (e.g., 'cardiac arrest', 'vasopressors', 'DNR', 'sepsis') versus spurious artifacts (timestamps, hospital-specific acronyms, discharge-related terms that leak label). Stratify by ethnicity to see if distinct vocabulary drives predictions across groups. Healthy signal: top features align with ICU severity indicators. Unhealthy signal: label-leakage terms (e.g., 'expired', 'morgue') or demographic-correlated slang.
possible_sources: Lundberg & Lee 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions (SHAP)' (NeurIPS 2017)
Caruana et al. 'Intelligible Models for HealthCare' (KDD 2015)
Boag et al. 'What's in a Note? Unpacking Predictive Value in Clinical Note Representations' (AMIA 2018)

probe_designs.content: For each test patient, swap the ethnicity one-hot vector to each of the other 5 groups, keeping clinical features fixed. Measure change in predicted mortality. Healthy signal: predictions invariant or minimally changed (<0.02 absolute probability shift) - indicating predictions driven by clinical content, not demographics. Unhealthy signal: large prediction shifts upon ethnicity swap, confirming direct demographic influence and violation of counterfactual fairness.
possible_sources: Kusner et al. 'Counterfactual Fairness' (NeurIPS 2017)
Garg et al. 'Counterfactual fairness in text classification through robustness' (AIES 2019)
Zhang et al. 'Hurtful words' (CHIL 2020)

probe_designs.content: Given the 10000-dim TF-IDF input, assess whether a simpler baseline (logistic regression with L1/L2, gradient boosting) matches or beats the pretrained clinical LM. Report parameter count, training time, inference latency, and AUROC. Compute effective rank of feature usage. Healthy signal: clinical LM shows meaningful lift (>2% AUROC) over sparse linear baselines justifying its cost. Unhealthy signal: simpler models match performance, suggesting LM capacity is wasted or TF-IDF discards the signal the LM could exploit.
possible_sources: van Aken et al. 'Clinical Outcome Prediction from Admission Notes' (EACL 2021)
Huang et al. 'ClinicalBERT' (CHIL 2020)
Alsentzer et al. 'Publicly Available Clinical BERT Embeddings' (ClinicalNLP 2019)

probe_designs.content: Quantify epistemic uncertainty via MC-dropout, deep ensembles, or bootstrapped models. Compare mean predictive entropy and variance across ethnicity groups. Healthy signal: uncertainty comparable across groups and higher for hard/ambiguous cases. Unhealthy signal: systematically higher variance for minority groups (signaling model is less confident due to undertraining on those cohorts), or overconfident wrong predictions for any subgroup. Also analyze selective prediction curves (risk-coverage) per group.
possible_sources: Gal & Ghahramani 'Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation' (ICML 2016)
Lakshminarayanan et al. 'Simple and Scalable Predictive Uncertainty Estimation Using Deep Ensembles' (NeurIPS 2017)
Dusenberry et al. 'Analyzing the Role of Model Uncertainty for Electronic Health Records' (CHIL 2020)
